segments.questionText: How many hours of sleep to you average per day?
segments.answerText: That's a good question. Probably like about 7 hours I would say is an average. I'm you know, I don't I'm not one of these don't sleep very much people. I need my sleep. If I'm if things are going well, it'll be 8 hours, honestly. And on some days of the week, depending on how things are going, I will take the 20-minute power nap in the middle of the day to keep things going. But yeah, about about 7 hours. Actually, like despite the uh pandemic and quarantine etc., I've been sleeping fine. Only one night recently did I have trouble sleeping. A lot of people are reporting that their dreams are more vivid than usual and I have been experiencing that. Apparently, it's some combination of you know, anxiety about the state of the world, but also just seeing the same environment around day after day, hour after hour. Not giving a lot a lot of different stimulation to your brain contributes to this vividness of your dreaming. So, I definitely am experiencing that.

segments.questionText: If the strong nuclear force is strong enough to hold together nuclei as heavy as uranium despite electromagnetic repulsion between protons, why aren’t there much heavier isotopes of light atoms, say carbon-50? Would a nucleus composed only of neutrons be stable?
segments.answerText: So, yeah, this is a very good question. You know, you you have this idea in mind that on the one hand protons, which are positively charged, push each other away cuz of the electromagnetic force, but nucleons, which include both protons and neutrons, pull each other together on very short distances. So, there's a competition, so some nuclei are stable and some are not. But, it's very natural to ask, well, why not just add more and more neutrons? So, you're increasing the strong nuclear force, but you are decreasing the electromagnetic repulsion. And the answer is because the neutron is not stable. The neutron's a little bit heavier than the proton, and a neutron all by itself is not stable at all. It decays in a matter of minutes into a proton, um, an electron, and an antineutrino. So, it turns out, just for complicated, you have to go through the calculation kind of reasons, that the same thing is true for just a bag of neutrons. If you try to make a nucleus out of many, many neutrons, that does not make them stable at all. So, uh, the more neutrons you put in, the more likely it is that those neutrons will just decay into protons, and those protons will be positively charged and pushing apart all the other protons. That's why you get this balance that gives us this interesting structure in the periodic table.

segments.questionText: What does a guy like you make per year?
segments.answerText: my what is my yearly income? So, uh, as Ezra surmises, maybe I'm not going to answer that question. Uh, you know, that is a personal question, but I get the point of it. Um, I'm, you know, as things go, I'm pretty successful. I I make I do fine. Let's put it that way. The joke that Jennifer and I have is the the one thing that we lack financially is the ability to take, you know, business class flights on very, very long travels uh, trips cuz for tall people these economy class flights these days, man, if you're on a 10-hour flight, it's tough. But, I can't quite afford the business class. Got to go in, uh, cargo with everybody else. Um, but, you know, I have I probably make less than the average tenured professor cuz I'm a research professor, which is less prestigious, but then I also have, um, the talking and the podcast and writing books, especially, does bring in extra income. So, I'm doing fine. Thanks for asking.

segments.questionText: what do you make of ideas like the Dyson sphere and other giant structures like that – do you think realistically it will ever be possible to build anything even remotely similar in scale?
segments.answerText: I think so. I mean, I don't see why not in principle. You know, there's always the question of the difference between violating the laws of physics versus doing crazily ambitious engineering projects, right? So, warp drive, going faster than the speed of light, things like that violate the laws of physics as we currently understand them. But, taking apart a planet and building a giant sphere, um it might be plausible. I really don't know. You know what I mean? There's certainly nothing in the laws of physics that would prevent it. Uh I think honestly the biggest difficulty would be disassembling a planet to get enough stuff to build a Dyson sphere. Maybe you could either blow it up, but then you run the danger of losing a lot of it. Uh probably the more sensible way is to send rockets down and then throw things up. Um you know, if it's a if it's a rocky planet, you can sort of uh land on it and, you know, throw rocks into space. Robert Heinlein talked about this in The Moon Is a Harsh Mistress. And then someone in space could collect those rocks and use them to build something like that. But, honestly, I have zero actual technical knowledge of this, so you shouldn't um believe my judgment very much, but that's what my judgment is.

segments.questionText: I’m hoping you can comment on Eric Weinstein’s theory of “Geometric Unity,” what it actually means in terms a layperson can understand.
segments.answerText: so, I cannot comment cuz I have no idea what is in Eric's theory. It's not really something that I've been following or very interested in.

segments.questionText: In John Wheeler’s book “Journey into Gravity and Spacetime” he say’s that if we were floating in space between galaxies we wouldn’t see any light from other stars or galaxies. Really! Is the space between galaxies so vast that it would be completely dark to our unaided eyes?
segments.answerText: on. Sorry, Judith, in this particular quote. And if you were in the space between galaxies, of course you would see other galaxies. You know, galaxies are faint, to be fair. The Andromeda galaxy is about as dim as the dimmest star that we can see with the naked eye. If you were in a If you picked a spot carefully in the universe, then all of the galaxies There'd be no galaxies close enough to you to be visible if you were if you sort of if the Earth were where you are, right? So, if you were looking through the Earth's atmosphere. Of course, if you're truly in outer space, you're not looking through the Earth's atmosphere, so things look a little bit brighter. So, in that sense, you probably still, I think, would be able to see galaxies. But, you know, the Andromeda galaxy is pretty close to us, so I guess as I'm as I'm saying this out loud, I'm I'm getting in mind what Wheeler probably had in mind. The Andromeda galaxy is big and it's relatively close to us, and yet it is approximately the bright the brightness brightness of the dimmest star visible with the naked eye. So, there's two competing effects. If you're in intergalactic space, you'd be farther away from all the galaxies, but you wouldn't have the Earth's atmosphere in the way. So, it's possible that he did the calculation, which I didn't do, which says that at the end of the day, the human eye would not be able to perceive any of the galaxies out there. So, that is that that is possible. Of course, all you need is good set of binoculars or a tiny telescope, and you would see all sorts of galaxies.

segments.questionText: Physicists have used two finely tuned optical lattice clocks, one at the base and one on the observatory floor (450 meters) of Tokyo Skytree, to make new ultraprecise measurements of the time dilation effect predicted by Einstein’s theory of general relativity. Time runs slightly more slowly at the base of the Skytree tower than at the top only because it’s only 450 meters tall. If it were significantly taller the top clock would dilate more because of the motion of the the Earth. Is there a name for “this point” (height of tower) at which the dilation from motion on the top level is equal to the dilation from the gravity well on the ground level?
segments.answerText: let me mention it because um it I think it's I think it comes up later in the AMA. I am never going to use phrases like the clock runs slower deep in a gravitational field or something like that. Clocks move at 1 second per second. What other speed could they possibly move at? There's a temptation to say that they move slower because you're imagining comparing them to clocks that are far away, but clocks that are far away can't be directly compared with clocks that are in a gravitational well or moving at the speed of light. That's part of the point of general relativity. All you can compare is the net amount of duration measured by two clocks that start at the same point, go off and travel somewhere, and then come back. But anyway, having said all that, um I have no idea what the different what the point at which the height of a tower and therefore the change in the gravitational potential wins or loses versus the speed at which things travel. So, the point here is that of course, compared if you if you could compare to someone uh that was far away from the Earth and just sitting stationary with respect to it, and then you both sent people off really fast and sent them deep into the Earth's gravitational field and compared how long it took their clocks to go different amounts when they came back, then there's competing effects because, you know, there's a speed of light effect and as you go through space at through space just a special relativistic effect, and then there's the gravitational time dilation. And so, David's question is when does the velocity at which you're moving become more important than the gravitational time dilation? The answer is I have no idea. I mean, you could do the calculation, uh but I have not done it. Sorry.

segments.questionText: If the past and future are not as Eddington said “As much available to present exploration as the distant parts of space.” then I don’t see how you can justify that they are equally real. You consider yourself an Eternalist but if the past isn’t always including now “out there” on some Spacetime world-point then what is eternal about your view?
segments.answerText: understanding the question because I think that Eddington's quote answers the question. I mean, Eddington is saying that the past and future are as much available to present exploration as the distant parts of space. So, an eternalist says that the past and future are equally real to distant parts of space. There's no difference between being distant in space and being distant in time, according to the block universe point of view. Um so, the only thing that I've said before and will say again is that they're not real now. You can't add these temporal modifiers, but that's okay. The whole point of the four-dimensional perspective is that you shouldn't add these temporal modifiers. You should just say, "They're real." Full stop.

segments.questionText: What do recent indications that different generation process for Type 1a supernovae may have differed in prevalence in the early universe and more recently the potential anisotropy in expansion imply for the hubble constant measurement discrepancy or the contribution of dark energy?
segments.answerText: really not able to give a detailed expert-level answer on this cuz I have not followed all of the recent uh claims and counterclaims about the Hubble constant discrepancy, the anisotropy, uh etc. There was a claim, for those of you who don't know, there was a claim recently that the expansion of the universe was anisotropic. Now, uh almost no professional cosmologist believes that claim because the measurement is done using galaxies, and you can do that. You can try to measure the isotropy of the universe using galaxies, but it's nowhere near as accurate or precise or as easy to measure as doing it using the cosmic microwave background. And we've done it using the cosmic microwave background, and it's isotropic. So, you're using a worse method with larger error bars uh and getting a claim that doesn't seem to make sense. Probably the claim's just not right. That's how Bayesian inference works. For the Hubble constant discrepancy, it's a it's a more interesting discrepancy. The people who are doing it know what they're doing and we don't know exactly what's going on. My bet is still that it's just going to go away when we understand the observations better, that it's not really new physics. That's my personal belief.

segments.questionText: Recently you tweeted out a blog post by Stephen Wolfram, where he writes excitedly about a new way of conceiving the fundamental laws of physics. I enjoyed his post, and I also took note of your wise words of caution about getting too excited. My question is: why would a scientist of stature opt to post something for public consumption without submitting it to formal peer-review first?
segments.answerText: plenty of scientists, including myself, submit things and make them publicly available before they've been formally peer-reviewed. That's the whole purpose of the Well, that's as a role that is played, maybe it's not the whole purpose, by the archive in high-energy physics and its corresponding There's a bio archive and a medical archive, etc. You can put out preprints, that is to say, papers you've written that have not yet been peer-reviewed, that usually will eventually be peer-reviewed, but you can get sort of a pre-peer-review peer-review by just putting them out there in public, letting your peers, the rest of the scientific community, look at them and comment and cite them and so forth. And if they're good, you know, use them even before they've appeared in a journal. So, the fact that it's not submitted in a peer-reviewed journal doesn't bother me at all. If it eventually is submitted to a peer-reviewed journal. But, the reason why I'm not that excited about it is, you know, again, people should go ahead and do it. Like, I'm not going to say don't do your crazy ideas about fundamental physics. To the people who are trying it, knock yourself out. Go nuts. To the people who are just on the sidelines asking how much should I care about this? My answer is you should not care about this kind of thing very much. Um it for lots of reasons, you know, the the there's a not just one but a million different things that go into creating an ambitious new picture of physics like this. And the ordinary thing to do is to say, well, I'm making 20 dramatically, incredibly implausible claims. Let me justify them one by one in a series of papers that can people can think about you know, lone geniuses just don't work as well as communities. And so real physicists put their stuff out there into the community and they talk about it and they get it better and they reference each other and they cite each other and they build upon what each other have done. So, if you say, you know, I have a discrete model that mimics quantum mechanics and satisfies Bell's theorem, and that's step three out of 20 in in making your theory of everything, well, jeez, that's a big enough thing. You should write a short paper about that and let people talk about that. And that's the kind of thing that makes me not that interested or not that give it gives me a very small prior on an approach like this being very useful is that it's just it it smells wrong, right? It like doesn't have any of the qualities that they're trying in good faith to address all of the big obsta- obvious obstacles to an approach like this. I mean, maybe they will, like I said, you know, there's some non-zero chance and if they do, then people will talk about it and people will realize it. But I would have much higher confidence if they just done it piecemeal in a way that said, uh you know, here's why we think we can do this, here's why we think we can do that, here's why we think we can do something else. I mean, the model is irreversible, it looks very non-local, it's discrete, it's not Lorenzen variant, it's doesn't look quantum mechanical at all. So, if someone says, but maybe it's right, all you have to do is spend the next three weeks of your life uh reading through the 800 pages they've written. Yeah, no, I'm not going to do that. You know, if you look on the archive I just talked about in particle physics and high energy theory, there are literally hundreds of papers coming out every week by physicists who are in the game, who are playing by the rules, who are putting papers out, letting other people comment on them, giving seminars and so forth. Why would I spend time listening to you know, someone's grandiose plans when I know these other people are doing their best to write papers in good faith and I don't even have time to read their papers. You know, I might have time to read one or two papers out of a hundred that actually get published there. So,

segments.questionText: Consider a three dimensional object falling through the plane of a two dimensional world — say a pencil falling point-first. To a two dimensional creature it would look like a point appearing out of nowhere, then widening into a disc, then disappearing. Is this in any way analagous to quantum particles materializing and disappearing – suggesting a source in a higher physical dimension?
segments.answerText: analogous to that. I I see where you're coming from, but the idea of quantum particles materializing and disappearing was always a wrong analogy. It was just a a sort of fake story that was told. If you want more of the lowdown on that, I go into some length about it in Something Deeply Hidden, my recent book. There's an appendix where I talk about Feynman diagrams and quantum fluctuations and so forth. But the point is that in quantum mechanics as we understand it, we have the Schrödinger equation, okay? And we don't have to go into the details about that, but the point is the universe evolves like a closed system. It evolves like you tell me what the quantum state is, the Schrödinger equation tells me how it evolves. Whereas the process that you're suggesting where something comes in from an extra dimension and looks mysterious to us, looks like it's popping into existence, that's because the plane into which the pencil is coming is not a closed system. It's being influenced by the outside world in some way. So, as best as we currently understand it, the laws of physics do not need any influence coming out from outside. So, that's why I don't think this is an especially good analogy uh for quantum fluctuations.

segments.questionText: Since forces allow one object to influence another object, and since entanglement allows one object to influence another object, is there a way to express entanglement and forces in terms of one another, or are they two completely different mechanisms by which an object can influence another object?
segments.answerText: I think the dirty secret here is that the word force is not actually well-defined in physics, or at least it's not uniquely defined in a way we can all agree on. In Newtonian mechanics, it was, but now we're talking about quantum mechanics, and things are a little bit more subtle. And furthermore, we're talking about field theory, cuz quantum field theory is our best theory of the world, where it's also more subtle than that. So, I think that it makes perfect sense not to mix together entanglement and forces, because the way that we have actually gone about in practice using the word force is to refer to, you know, the famous four forces of physics: gravity, electromagnetism, um the strong and weak nuclear forces. You can also add the Higgs force onto that. It's very, very short-range, but in principle, it's there. And so, in practice, what we mean is a bosonic field, okay, that can either pull or push other fields together, and it work working completely locally. Entanglement is something uh utterly different than that, right? It Entanglement is not mediated by a field. Um it does seem to be an influence, whether or not you want to call it a force, but people disagree, depending on whether they're many-worlders or mul- uh hidden-variable people or something else, about how entanglement and the measurement process actually works. Uh but in none of their views do you need a force to make it happen. So, it's probably not a good thing to mix those two up. John Hammel says, "You You the idea that free will skeptics are simply not being consistent unless they act in a really asocial and sociopathic way. So, parenthetically, I have never said that. You say I discussed the idea. Well, someone else uses those words and I'll discuss those words, but those are not my words just to be clear.

segments.questionText: You’ve discussed the idea that free will skeptics “are simply not being consistent unless they act in a really asocial … and sociopathic way”. I’m a free will skeptic. I think that the wet-meat computer in my head attempts to calculate the outcomes of various decisions; and that evolutionary influences and other factors give me a preference for outcomes that benefit me and society. Is there any part of this perspective that implies I should behave like a sociopath in order to be consistent?
segments.answerText: and evolution etc. gives you preference for outcomes. And I also think that you should talk about your decision-making process using free will choice-based vocabulary. The point at which you become inconsistent is when you say, well, I refuse to talk about human choices and volition and free will, but I still want to use words like should and morally right and wrong and responsibility and blame and things like that. I think that is inconsistent. I think you should choose either to use words like responsibility, blame, morally right and wrong along with words like choices and volition or use none of those words. Uh if you can't make choices, then there's no sense in which your actions are right or wrong. It just doesn't quite make sense. So, an analogy to me is imagine that Thanos from the Marvel Cinematic Universe takes Captain America uh at the top of the tall building and throws Captain America off the building. And Captain America does not have the ability to fly for those of you familiar with the Marvel Cinematic Universe. And Cap ends up uh landing on the ground and landing right on an innocent bystander who's there by immediately killed. So, would we say that Captain America has made a morally wrong action? That Captain America is to blame for what happened? I think we would say no, because there's once we have the initial conditions that Captain America has been thrown out the window or thrown off the roof of a tall building, there's nothing that that given that given that information, there's nothing that Captain America can do to prevent him from falling onto the ground and and killing that person who doesn't have the ability to move out fast enough, right? Uh the laws of physics have uh declared that that's what's going to happen. But what if we say, do we assign moral blame to Thanos's action in throwing Captain America out the window or off the roof? Well, yes. And the reason why that's a sensible thing to do is because given what we know, given the conditions that we are actually aware of, like there Thanos is there, Thanos is a thinking being with the ability to reason, and we can talk to him and try to make him make choices and so forth, given all that information, he could have acted differently, right? He has the ability, you know, given what we know, it's perfectly consistent to imagine different futures. Now, you can counterfactually say, but if we were Laplace's demon and knew all the atoms that Thanos is made of and everybody else, we could have predicted it. Fine. But we're not. And that's why, rather than talking the language of I just saw the wave function of the universe every day, we talk a language of people making choices and assigning blame and responsibility to the outcomes of those choices.

segments.questionText: I can understand how the curvature of space causes objects to follow a curved path around a center of mass, such as the planets orbiting the Sun. What I’m having trouble understanding is how the curvature of space imparts on those objects an acceleration towards the center of mass. What am I missing?
segments.answerText: missing the fact that I don't know whether you're missing this or not, but the fact is that curvature uh is gravity, right? I mean, so there maybe what you're asking is um why? Uh you know, I guess maybe I'm trying to read the question. I'm trying to figure out what exactly you're asking. There is a question that can be asked, which is why do uh particles or objects moving through curved space-time look like they're being pulled by gravity, right? So, on the one hand, there's the question why do particles moving through curved space-time follow what we call geodesics, shortest distance paths through space-time. And that's something you can derive from the laws of physics, right? You don't just need to posit it. You can just posit it also. In fact, in the philosophy of general relativity community, there are arguments over whether or not geodesic motion is postulated or derived. But anyway, that is part of Einstein's general theory of relativity. Um the other part is so why is that action, why are particles moving along geodesics similar to what you and I think of as gravity, in particular why is there an acceleration toward the center of mass? Well, the center of mass curves space-time around it in a particular way, and then you do the math. I mean, I'm not quite sure what level you need a uh answer to this question. I can do the math. I've written a book. You can write You can read uh space-time and geometry. You take Einstein's equation for the curvature of space-time in the presence of some mass. You solve it. You get something for a spherically symmetric mass that looks like the Schwarzschild solution, and then you look at the geodesics in the Schwarzschild solution, and they fall, right? Or they get bent. They get uh uh deflected as they as they move by a mass. I'm not quite sure what other level um you're looking for an answer to this question. Sorry about that.

segments.questionText: are the gravitational waves carrying oscillations in several directions transverse to their path of propagation like the electro and magnetic fields that travel together in the electromagnetic wave?
segments.answerText: polarization, and then there's electric versus magnetic fields. So, in an electromagnetic wave, what you have is there's a vibrations in both the electric field and the magnetic field, and those vibrations uh are vertical and perpendicular to the line of motion, okay? So, if there's a motion of a of a beam of light, an electromagnetic wave, then the fluctuations in the electric field are perpendicular to the momentum of that line of photons or whatever it is. And the oscillations in the magnetic field are perpendicular to those of the electric field, okay? So, since there's only three dimensions of space and three perpendicular lines, the momentum, the electric field, and the magnetic field, uh they better all be mutually perpendicular to each other, and there's no degree of freedom other than, you know, pick one and then we know the others. Uh actually, pick two and then we know the other three. So, if you tell me the momentum, you tell me the electric field, I know what the magnetic field is doing. So, the polarization is just the direction in which the electric field is pointing. Vertical polarized means the electric field is vibrating up and down in some reference frame. Horizontally polarized, it's oscillating left and right. Then there's complex uh circular polarization and things like that. But you only need The fact that the magnetic field is perpendicular to the electric field is separate from the fact that there are polarizations that are either vertical or horizontal. Now, for the gravitational field, we can, but sometimes don't, but sometimes do, think about the electric and the magnetic part of the gravitational field. It is a slightly confusing nomenclature because it has nothing to do with electromagnetism, but in the gravitational wave, there is a part of it that has the features that resemble the electric field, and there's a part of it that has features that resemble the magnetic field. So, again, you can buy my general relativity book and see the different components of gravitational wave expressed in terms of an electric-like part and a magnetic-like part, just like an electromagnetic wave. And also, just like an electromagnetic wave, that's separate from the polarization question. Uh since a gravitational wave has sort of a plus or X-shaped polarization, it's still two polarizations, but they look different than the electromagnetic polarization. So, there's a close analogy here, but they're different things.

segments.questionText: I understand that E=MC2 relates mass to energy and that the speed of light is a factor. What I don’t understand is why C is squared.
segments.answerText: squared as opposed to I guess, you know, to the first power, to the third power, whatever." Um you know, part of this This is again one of those questions where the answer There's a perfectly respectable, sensible answer, which is that's just the way it is. Those are the laws of physics, okay? Now, you can, you know, when you ask these why questions, you can always say, "Well, what What are you going to grant me? What postulates do I get to start with to derive this fact that you want?" Uh so, one way of doing it, very, very simple-minded, but it works, is just to use dimensional analysis, right? Uh if you if you think about the units of these quantities, energy, mass, and velocity, you know that the kinetic energy is 1/2 mv squared. So, you know that the units in which energy is measured, whatever they are, joules or ergs or what have you, are the dimensions the units of mass times the dimension of velocity squared, cuz it's 1/2 mv squared and 1/2 doesn't have dimensions at all. So, if you if someone told you that they had a formula, energy equals mass times the speed of light to some power, and they said, "What is the power going to be?" you'd instantly know it has to be squared just to get the units right. Now, you can also derive E = mc squared in special relativity in ways where the c squared comes from the form of the Lorentz transformation, but then of course you're assuming special relativity to begin with. So, it all depends on what you're allowing yourself to assume.

segments.questionText: My question has to do with ‘first principles’ – Can you define the word “perpendicular” without referring to 90 degree angles or similar concepts which seem to be human-invented? Is there an a priori meaning of “perpendicular” which arises wholly from the fundamental laws of physics, and which would apply for all succeeding spatial dimensions?
segments.answerText: absolutely is. You don't Nothing is human invented here. This is geometry. Um, and it depends, you know, as as before, it depends on how precise you want to be and what postulates you want to start with. Let's imagine that uh, we have a background geometry which we agree is Euclidean, okay? In other words, um, Pythagoras' theorem is true, initially parallel lines remain parallel, that kind of thing, Euclid's laws, but in any number of dimensions. We'll allow it to be any number of dimensions. Then, if you take, for example, a dot on a line or a dot on a plane, I can draw a circle around that dot. Uh, if the dot is on a plane, then the circle is, um, one-dimensional, right? This is a one-dimensional line that goes in a circle around the dot. If the dot is on a line, a circle is zero-dimensional. A zero-dimensional circle is embedded in a one-dimensional line. It's just the two points. It's just a set of two points equidistant from the initial point that you drew, okay? So, now, if you go perpendicular, if you draw a line starting from that initial point and go perpendicularly, it's just the set of points that are equidistant from the circle, okay? The set of points that have the same distance from all the points on the circle. So, if you have just one dot on a line and then your quote-unquote circle is two equidistant points around it, then if you if you ask yourself, what is the set of all points that are equally distant from those two points that are equally distant from the original point that you started with, you would end up drawing a line that bisects and is perpendicular to the original line. Likewise, if you had a point on a plane with a little circle around it and ask the question, what is the set of points which are equidistant from all points on that circle you just drew, you would again draw a line that is perpendicular to that plane. So, you're not using any human-invented notions at all. You have to use notions like distance and um planes and points and lines, but that's part of the fundamental vocabulary of geometry. It's not a human uh it's not it's not subjective, let's put it that way.

segments.questionText: How close to discovering Einstein’s special relativity were Lorentz and Poincaré? How far beyond them did Einstein go? How was Poincaré’s relativity of simultaneity different from Einstein’s?
segments.answerText: So, these are all great questions, and I'm not really an expert on the history enough to give you a definitive answer here. Um a great book you can read is Einstein's Clocks and Poincaré's Maps by Peter Galison, where he talks about not only these questions, but also the fact that both Poincaré and Einstein were very much influenced by their day jobs. Uh Einstein looking at clocks in the patent office and Poincaré looking at maps in the Bureau of Longitude uh for the French government. Um I think very roughly the story that we tell ourselves, which might have some truth to it, is that the math was all there uh before Einstein came along. The math of special relativity, the Lorentz transformations, like you say, the relativity of simultaneity, people like Lorentz and Fitzgerald and Poincaré had all put that together. But what they how the way that they thought of it was different. That's really what Einstein added. Um what they thought was that there really was a Newtonian absolute space and absolute time. But then there was a set of ways that things transformed so that everything you could never measure what that absolute space and time were. And they called it the ether, and they they imagined that you could sensibly talk about your velocity with respect to the ether, but you just couldn't measure it. And Einstein was the one who came along and said, "Actually, well, if that's true, I can invent just as good a theory without the ether at all. I can just abandon the ether, and what you need to make that make sense is a different notion of what length means and what duration means, how time and space are measured. And it was eventually Minkowski who was able to say, you know, yeah, let's meld them together into one four-dimensional space-time.

segments.questionText: You’ve said that when you first encountered Bell’s Theorem it wasn’t the kind of epiphanic moment for you that it has been for some other physicists. Could you say more about this? Do you think Bell’s discovery has been oversold?
segments.answerText: say more about it. I can say the same the things I always say about it, which is the uh importance of Bell's theorem to how one thinks about quantum mechanics depends a lot on how one thinks about quantum mechanics. So if you think that we don't know how quantum mechanics really works. If you think that uh quantum mechanics remains mysterious, that we can eventually figure it out, but right now we're still striving toward that. We're trying to build the correct theory of quantum mechanics, then Bell's theorem is incredibly important. Bell's theorem is an incredibly strong constraint on the ways you might imagine making uh physical theories that match the predictions of quantum mechanics. In particular, if you're going to have hidden variables in your theories and they better be non-local one way or the other. Now, on the other hand, if you think you basically know what quantum mechanics is, like I do, like it's the many worlds version, like the Everettian quantum mechanics, and you think that the interesting work to be done in the foundations of quantum mechanics is the very difficult and very unfinished job of mapping Everettian quantum mechanics onto the world that we see. That's a hard thing to do, which we haven't completely finished yet. Then Bell's theorem is just not important. It just doesn't play a role. Like Bell's theorem is just true in Everettian quantum mechanics because it's a theorem and it's true and and Everettian in mechanics is consistent with the data. Uh there are assumptions that Bell goes into making his predictions, and one of the assumptions is that experimental outcomes have unique experimental experiments, I should just say, have unique outcomes. But in many worlds they don't. In many worlds experiments have different outcomes in different worlds. So Bell's theorem is still mathematically true as a theorem, it's just not relevant for many worlds. So I think it depends on what your perspective is there.

segments.questionText: If I have two (disjoint) intervals on a world line, how would I pick out a clock that can reliably tell me if the two intervals have the same duration (or tell me their duration ratios)? That is, how would I decide which recurrent processes are periodic?
segments.answerText: So I think this is a good question. I've addressed it before. I think it's it's sort of not one that physicists generally think about in part because we kind of build in the answers to our theories. It's just a fact of relativity that there's a way of measuring time that there are things that there's something called the space-time interval, and there's a way of physical there's a set of physical ways of measuring that space-time interval using clocks. But if you want to ask the deeper question, why is there any such way? Why why do we have the confidence that we can measure time using physical objects? Then what I'd like to say is that it it's the empirical fact that the world is full of clocks. That is to say, it's the the world is full of objects that do things repetitively, cyclically, over and over again in a way that when you compare them to each other, they take the same amount of time. So it takes the same amount of time for the Earth to revolve around the Sun and rotate its around its axis comparing to each other every single year. Different atomic transitions with different frequencies can be compared to each other. Pendulums rocking back and forth can be compared to each other. The universe is just full of clocks. And so as far as we can tell, that's enough to actually say that yes, these clocks taken together, their mutual consistency guarantees that they are reliable clocks, and then we can use them to measure time even along completely disjoint intervals. Um in other words, let's say yet another way. Uh let's imagine that Thanos comes back to us again, and he snaps his fingers, and instead of killing half of the living beings in the universe, he slows down time by a factor of two everywhere. In every single process, okay? Literally everything slows down by a factor of two. Well, that's exactly the same as doing nothing. It's completely meaningless slowing down time by a factor of two because everything slows down. And you would still have the fact that clocks are completely compatible with each other, and they would measure the same amount of time along different clocks. So, that kind of difference is just completely meaningless in physics.

segments.questionText: Can you explain how dimensions in Hilbert space relate to the wave function and the measurement problem?
segments.answerText: Um I think the answer is no, and I I suspect, but I might be being unfair here, so correct me Sorry about if I am being unfair here, Doug, but I think you might be misinterpreting the word dimensions. Remember, dimensions to to scientists and mathematicians just mean a direction. If you have a vector space, a space of things you can add together and scale by real real numbers, the dimension just the number of basis vectors, okay? It's not a place you can go or anything like that. So, the When you talk about the wave function, the wave function is a vector in a big vector space. That vector space is called Hilbert space. So, there's a very direct relationship between the number of dimensions of Hilbert space and the wave function, namely that the wave function is a vector in a in a vector space of that many dimensions. The measurement problem, I don't think it has I mean, it it the dimensionality of Hilbert space has nothing special to do with the measurement problem except in so far as it's part of quantum mechanics and quantum mechanics has to do with the measurement problem. So, I think I might be misunderstanding your question, but that's the best I can do right now.

segments.questionText: I’m confused by the different explanations given by physicists regarding what space is made of. Is it purely a mathematical/geometrical construct and therefore has no physical constituent(s), or does it (the “fabric”) comprise of something physical such that it can be “loopy” / “frothy” at the Planck scale?
segments.answerText: have no idea what space is really made of or even if there is an answer to that question, right? Because uh once you start talking about loopiness, frothiness, Planck scale, you're talking about quantum gravity and we don't know what the right theory of quantum gravity is. So, you should be confused cuz we don't know the answer to that question. Classically, in general relativity, space or space-time more properly is just one of the fundamental ingredients. It's not made of anything else. It's one of the things that you posit to exist and you can talk about its properties, its geometry, how it changes with time, things like that. But in classical physics, space isn't made of anything. In quantum mechanics, we just don't know.

segments.answerText: So, I think this um I think we we talked similar questions in the um last AMA and it it's a Pascal's wager kind of argument in my mind. You're saying, "Sure, this little thing is very, very unlikely, but its consequences are enormously big, so shouldn't it really override all of our other considerations?" But the problem with these really unlikely things is that there's a lot of really unlikely things. So, you say, if I don't get a regular technical checkup of my car, then there's an infinite number of universes where I hit somebody and kill them by accident. But there's also an infinite number of universes where, because I didn't get a checkup on my car, uh the car breaks down and I stopped the trip I was taking and I don't kill somebody. So, I've saved an infinite number of lives as well. And the point is that you got to take seriously the fact that these things are unlikely. There's a way, way bigger infinity where nothing special happens because of that technical checkup you did or did not have. And that is the going to be the one that dominates uh your consideration. You can't just say, I have a big number times a tiny number, therefore the result is big. If the tiny number is tiny enough, the result is still tiny even if the big number is big. There you go. All right. Zanich Consler says, I've

segments.questionText: I’ve listened to your Higgs Boson and Beyond lectures and the Something Deeply Hidden audiobook, and I hear “renormalization” come up as a pivotal solution to some nasty problems involving infinities. I’m wondering: where do these infinities come from, why are they troublesome, and how does renormalization pacify them?
segments.answerText: how does renormalization pacify them? So, the infinities in quantum field theory come about because we have a very specific way of doing quantum field theory, and that's because uh quantum field theory is hard. Okay? This sounds vague, but it'll get specific in a second. Um when we do quantum field theory, we generally start classically, right? We start with the classical field and then we quantize it. And in we do more than that. Not only do we start with the classical field and quantize it, we start with a free field. So, what we mean by that is you have, you know, the electron field, the quark fields, the electromagnetic field, etc. Have all these fields, and they're all interacting with each other in complicated ways that make it almost impossible to say exactly what's going on. So, the technique used by physicists is to say, "Okay, first, imagine that all these fields exist, but by hand, turn off all their interactions." That's what it means to be a free field. They're just freely evolving all by themselves. They don't bump into each other. They don't notice each other, okay? Then, you can solve the equations exactly. Everything's great. That's easy. Of course, it's completely uninteresting. You have literally no interactions between anything. So, then you start turning on the interactions, and you treat them as small, and you say, "Okay, I now have I went from zero interaction to just a little bit of interaction between my fields. What happens?" And this first answer you get in this approximation where the interaction is weak is everything's great. You know, the fields sort of you have a wave packet of one field and a wave packet of another field representing two particles. They can bump into each other. You can calculate the probability that they go separate ways, and so forth. But then you can try to improve this calculation by adding another level of approximation, and everything falls apart. That's where you get the infinities. In particular, because when you allow different wavelengths of intermediate states between the initial fields that you had bumping into each other, there's an infinite number of wavelengths that you can include. You could go from, you know, whatever tiny number you start with cuz the particles are small all the way down to zero, zero wavelength. And as you probably know, in quantum mechanics, small wavelengths means high energies. So, you're going from regular energies up to infinitely big energies, and so you get infinitely big corrections to your problems. Now, renormalization is the technique that was developed to get rid of these infinities, but it's critically important to realize the infinities aren't there in nature. The infinities aren't part of quantum field theory. The infinities are because we human beings are sloppy and bad at solving the equations of quantum field theory. So, we use this weird way of putting everything together, okay? So, the infinities are basically a way Renormalization is basically a way of keeping the infinities that we started with, but subtracting off another infinity that we put in to get rid of this new infinity. And it does sound like you're cheating, and you know, Feynman and and Schwinger and so forth who invented these techniques uh expressed skepticism about their own techniques through the rest of their lives. But these days, it doesn't bother us that much because we realize that all of these infinities came not only from we human beings being sloppy about solving our theories, but also from taking seriously infinitely big energy wavelengths, right? Very tiny wavelengths corresponding to infinite energy particles. And that's just not realistic, you know, they probably aren't there, all these things that are giving us the infinities. So, this is why Ken Wilson, who passed away a couple years ago, uh is a hero to modern physicists cuz he's the one who really understood, following work by others, but he's really the one who understood how if you what you can do is renormalize not by taking infinity minus infinity, but just by explicitly admitting that your theory is only supposed to be good up to a certain wavelength. And then you can use that that wavelength that energy as a way of parameterizing everything that could be going on that you don't know about at higher energies, and you can construct what is called an effective field theory. So, these days, modern approaches to renormalization admit that everything we are talking about in the real world is an effective field theory, and it's only good up to some energy scale, and we show that the physical predictions of the theory don't depend on what that energy scale is, and that's the renormalization group. So, roughly speaking, the very short answer to your question is renormalization is a way of treating what happens at really, really short wavelengths and high energies in terms of its impact on what we see at low energies and long wavelengths and bundling it up into all uh sort of way that we can handle it very effectively.

segments.questionText: Is there a short answer to “How does simply having the wave function of the universe evolving according to the Schroedinger equation lead directly and unambiguously to an Everettian many-worlds universe”?
segments.answerText: Um, I don't know how short you want, but the answer is through initial conditions and decoherence. Initial conditions because the arrow of time plays an important role in Everedian quantum mechanics just as it does in statistical mechanics. In statistical mechanics, we start out with a low entropy universe and entropy goes up. In Everett in many worlds, we start with a world with a wave function that has very few branches and the number of branches increases. And furthermore, we start in in the most straightforward way of talking about it, we start with a division into macroscopic systems like you and your measuring apparatus and your cats and so forth. And then an environment with a bazillion degrees of freedom that we don't keep track of, right? The environment has the photons and the air molecules and all that stuff. And gradually over time, the wave function of the system becomes more and more entangled with the wave function of the environment and that leads to decoherence because the environment states that are associated with different system states become perpendicular to each other and that makes them essentially separate worlds. They evolve completely independently from each other. They do not influence each other. There's no more interference experiments you could imagine doing where one world would affect the other. I have no idea whether or not that those small number of words I just said make any sense. If you want the longer version, I can recommend a really good book about quantum mechanics where I go into it in great detail.

segments.questionText: my son just recently heard of Eric Weinstein’s Gravity Unification theory. Since the subject is rearing its ugly head again, can you please just briefly explain your opinion of it?

segments.questionText: When we speak of cosmic occurrences e.g. specific fundamental forces separating out 10 ^ – [ ] s after the Big Bang, what “Big Clock” are we referring to, given that time intervals are not absolute?
segments.answerText: there's sort of two aspects to this question. One is the one we already talked about, you know, how do we know there are clocks, right? And the answer there is that we still know the fundamental physical processes. We know them pretty well. Just a a second or tiny fraction of a second after the Big Bang, so we know how long things took. And we can even check our work using things like the light element abundances left over from the Big Bang, and we find that we got pretty much the right answer. So, we think we're on the right track. The other aspect is, of course, there are different trajectories you can move on, and therefore different amounts of time measured by different clock, right? Just using the rules of relativity. And that answer is just a very boring one that the matter in the universe gives us a rest frame, right? Gives us a frame with respect to which we can say we are at rest with respect to on average the other stuff in the universe. And you can say, "Well, I could choose to move at some high velocity with respect to that rest frame." Knock yourself out. But what we mean when we say 10 to the minus 10 seconds or 1 minute or 100,000 years after the Big Bang is as measured by a clock that is at rest with respect to the average motion of stuff in the universe.

segments.answerText: Um so, I'm not So, again, I'm I'm just uh this is a hard question for me to answer because I don't know what your background knowledge is. segments.answerText: Um well, I mean, you can try, right? But, the problem is that we're bad at visualizing things that are even three-dimensional, right? Because you have to imagine sort of seeing into them. They have to be transparent or translucent or something like that. It happens all the time in physics that we visualize complicated things by simplifying down to one or two dimensions. Even Hilbert space, which plausibly has at least a dimensionality of 10 to the power 10 to the power 122, is often approximated by being two-dimensional. We work with qubits, right? That are two-dimensional. So, I I I suppose I could try to come up with a better visualization, but honestly, my real answer is two-dimensional visualizations are fine. That's There's nothing wrong with that. We are constantly simplifying things, and there's a famous XKCD cartoon where, you know, he gets at that. He, you know, there's a teacher writing on the blackboard, the rubber sheet analogy, and someone raises their hand and says like, "I don't like the rubber sheet analogy cuz they it's gravity that is pulling down the ball on the rubber sheet, but you're supposed to be explaining gravity." segments.questionText: Do you think that some of the answers concerning the nature of consciousness can be partially answered within the realm of quantum mechanics in physics?
segments.answerText: think so. I talked a little bit about this in something deeply hidden and a little bit about this also in The Big Picture. I'm a physicalist. I think that consciousness is just an emergent phenomenon from the laws of physics. There is a good physics question which people like Matthew Fisher at UC Santa Barbara have tackled, um namely is a classical model of the brain sufficient to capture its actual physical behavior or do we need to take into account quantum entanglement and things like that? I mean, after all, the the brain is made of atoms, made of particles, made of things that obey the rules of quantum mechanics. Quantum entanglement might be important. But also, the brain is a is a wet noisy system, which is exactly where we should expect everything to decohere very quickly in the classical approximation to be very good. So, I don't think that consciousness in particular has anything to do with quantum mechanics at all.

segments.questionText: Could you explain an experiment that would test Many-Worlds vs Copenhagen in QM?
segments.answerText: because Copenhagen is not well defined. I have no idea what is meant by the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics. The problem with Copenhagen is you say, you know, I measure an object and its wave function collapses, but you never say what you mean by measuring. You never say, you know, what it means, how quickly it happens. Many worlds is completely rigorously defined. It says that the Schrödinger equation is true at every moment. If you give me a version of the Copenhagen interpretation that is equally uh well defined, that tells me exactly when wave functions collapse, uh that would be something that we could test because for one thing, unless you have hidden variables or something else going on, the energy of the wave function changes when you do a experiment in quantum in quantum measurement in the Copenhagen interpretation. So, energy is not conserved in the Copenhagen interpretation. In many worlds, it's more subtle because energy is overall conserved in the in wave function of the universe, but branch by branch it might not be. So, that kind of thing might very well like you do an experiment. But, until one of the two competitors is well-defined, I cannot test them against each other.

segments.questionText: A thought experiment: an observer is inside a satellite no windows, in orbit around a gravity well. In the room is a computer terminal, connected to a main tower outside the room by a cable…let’s say it’s running Minecraft. When the observer begins to play, they notice the framerate is lagging abnormally. Would there be any way for that observer to tell whether the lag is caused by the main tower being lowered into the gravity well and being subjected to time dilation or, rather, by another person in the room next door trying to run another instance of the program at the same time? If not, would this represent another equivalence principle, similar to the equivalence between gravity and acceleration? And what might be the implications of that?
segments.answerText: can shorten it down is I'm in a I'm in a locked spaceship, but I have a computer that is connected to the outside world. The computer seems to be running slowly. Can I tell the difference between it's running slowly because of general relativity versus it's running slowly because someone is soaking up CPU cycles on the server? I don't think that you necessarily could tell the difference between that. And but I don't think it's another equivalence principle. I think it's the equivalence principle. You know, I think it this is saying that you know, it's it's a slightly a slightly roundabout argument, but if the only thing Let me see Let me me the best way to say this is. Um, you can use the equivalence principle to derive the fact that something that is far away uh experiences time differently than something that is at your point in the gravitational potential well. And from that you could derive this result. That's all you need. All you're using here is the fact that there is gravitational time dilation. Uh, and the simple way of saying your question is, can you distinguish gravitational time dilation just from a system slowing down for some other reason? And the answer is no. But I think that's the ordinary equivalence principle, not a new one.

Gödel, Escher, Bach was a classic that I read when I was a kid. Um, George Gamow's books, like One, Two, Three, Infinity, were other ones that I read when I was a kid. Um, Jana Levin uh wrote her first book called How the Universe Got Its Spots. I really have enormous admiration for cuz it's very different. It uses physics, but it's also sort of a personal quasi novelistic kind of story. So, there's a bunch of books out there that are that are really good in different ways. Um I'm trying to think like all of the people who have appeared on my podcast have written good books. So, I don't don't feel bad if I haven't mentioned your name uh by explicitness.

segments.questionText: What are your thoughts on the tension between individual rights and economic growth versus public health that we’re seeing play out throughout the country in response to the Coronavirus crisis? It seems that stay-at-home orders are really the only effective tool we currently have right now, but an outspoken minority of the public and politicians are growing impatient.
segments.answerText: know, I think that there is there's a lot going on here. One is that there is, of course, in principle uh a fascinating tug-of-war between um different kinds of social goals, like health versus wealth, okay? Roughly speaking. Um in practice, I don't think this is an example of this. I think that, you know, a large number of people dying would be really bad for the economy. So, I think that both the health and the wealth criteria both point to saying keep the country shut down for a while until we're sure that we have uh dealt with the problem as well as we can. And then, even when we reopen it, be ready to shut it down again if we see uh things flare up once again. Obviously, what we're hoping for is a future regime where we can test people enormously quickly and in enormous numbers so that we know when things are safe. Uh even better would be a vaccine or something like that where people could think that they personally are are very safe. Um so, right now I don't think that realistically there's much actual debate going on. There there is that the other thing on the other side, the way in which we reopen is a very difficult question, right? You know, so you can say, "All right, the number of new cases per day is seen to be going down. That's good. Uh we've we've been pretty good about being responsible, and so things are getting better. Let's start coming back to life a little bit, you know, let's let uh hardware stores open or um restaurants, you know, but should you still have concerts and things like that? And I I think that you can't predict ahead of time is the most well-established fact. You can't predict ahead of time what will happen when you do these things. So, when you do them, I think we should go slowly and patiently. Um I don't think that there are that many people who are growing impatient. There's just a very small number that are noisy, and I'm very happy to ignore those people.

segments.questionText: When you’re not an expert on a topic but want to have an informed opinion, what is your strategy for developing that opinion?
segments.answerText: Yeah, that's a is a really important question. You know, I think that I am a believer in expertise and knowledge. So, I look at people who are experts uh and do have knowledge, and but by that I mean, you know, people who have a track record, who have established within that field that they have accomplished something, they know about it, they've uh spoken about it, and maybe even contributed to it. I don't necessarily believe them uh just because they are experts, but if I have two people with opinions, one of them is an expert and one is not, I will put more credence on the expert's opinion than on the non-expert's opinion. Um there's a there are higher-level considerations that come into mind beyond whether a person is an expert or not. You can kind of try to tell by how people talk about things, how trustworthy they are. By By which I mean, you can be a super-duper expert in something, but you can also be a zealot, right? You can be a fanatic. You can be dedicated to your particular take on that thing and be really, really, really good at it, and yet not be trustworthy. As Ezra Klein said on the podcast I did with him, you know, nobody knows more about uh the architecture of buildings and how they can collapse than 9/11 truthers. Because they're they're obsessed with this stuff. They're utterly unreliable about it, but they're incredibly knowledgeable about it. So, you have to sort of convolve your judgment of whether or not someone is an expert with your judgment of their judgment. Do they seem to be the kind of person who says, "Well, here's what I think, but I'm not completely sure. Here's the evidence I have, but I'm still learning." versus the kind of person who says, "I understand this. No one else understands this. Everyone else is an idiot. I have a new way of doing things that is radically different, and only my genius is good enough to comprehend it." That kind of person I don't put much stake in at all.

segments.answerText: to say about Stephen Wolfram, et cetera.

segments.questionText: Do you think about anthropic reasoning is a valuable intellectual tool?
segments.answerText: Uh yeah, you know, in very, very specific circumstances. So, it's not I think there's there's sort of a a very trivial obvious level at which it's useful, and there's a more subtle level at which I'm not quite sure I understand it, but I think I'm coming closer. The trivial level is this. Um There are selection effects in where people and intelligent life forms appear in the universe. So, if someone thought it was a really good question to ask, "Why in the world did life arise on the surface of the Earth rather than the surface of the Sun?" Given that the Sun has a much bigger surface, you would expect it is much more likely that life would arise on the surface of the Sun. Well, I think that you there's a perfectly good anthropic answer to that. Namely, that the surface of the Sun might be big, but it's not very hospitable to the existence of life. Therefore, I have answered that question. Therefore, that question is in the pile of questions I'm not going to worry about anymore, right? So, I think that's a very trivial, very true, very robust use of anthropic reasoning. Now, where it becomes much more problematic and difficult, and not necessarily wrong, but just, you know, legitimately difficult and requiring care, is where you want to say, um, "What can I conclude about the wider universe from my place in it?" So, um, if there is, uh, universe where there's a set of very different conditions, like a multiverse, whether it's different branches of the wave function or different pocket universes in a cosmology, um, how should I reason probabilistically about what I expect to see in such a universe? There is sort of the straightforward idea that I should just imagine that it's most likely that I'm a typical observer in that ensemble, right? And I could even extend this to time as well as space. So, I should be a typical observer in the history of humanity, right? If I'm given that I'm human, conditionalizing on the fact that I'm a human being, we should be typical human beings. That That sort of has a sweetly reasonable character to it, right? Like, of course we're typical. That seems very humble and modest. And this leads to arguments like the There's a famous thing called the Doomsday argument, which says that humanity won't last too much longer. I mean, too much might be, you know, a few hundred, uh, thousand years, but not millions or billions of years. Why? Well, because if humanity lasts a million years, then we are not typical. We are really right near at the beginning. But, if you think we're neither in the first 5% of all the human beings who ever lived, nor the last 5%, then that bounds where we are in the history of humanity. I think that's complete nonsense, to be honest. Um, because there's no reason to think that we're typical observers within humanity. It sounds very humble, but it's anti-humble, because you're getting all your leverage not from saying you are typical, but from saying typical people are like you. And that's not humble at all. I talk a little bit about this in uh, my paper on why Boltzmann brains are bad. It's called the presumptive philosopher problem. And I'm actually, you know, I have some notes for another paper that I might write someday about this about anthropic reasoning more generally. My favorite paper ever written about anthropic reasoning is by Redford Neal, n e a l, who is a statistician at the University of Toronto. And it's called Puzzles of Anthropic Reasoning um solved. I forget the exactly, but solved or something like that by fully non-indexical conditioning. So he takes the opposite point of view that rather than thinking that you're a typical observer, you should you should take into account all of what you already know about what kind of observer you are. And that's the only anthropic reasoning you're allowed to do. I'm not 100% on board with this, but I think it's much better than the typical paper on this stuff out there.

segments.questionText: In the Carroll-Chen 2004 model of baby universes being born with low entropy, the babies spring from random fluctuations of the vacuum in the parent universe. Is there any way to avoid also getting lots of Boltzmann Brains from similar fluctuations? Can we have this explanation of our universe’s low entropy, and also be reasonably confident we aren’t Boltzmann Brains?
segments.answerText: the answer is yes. I think this is exactly one of the benefits of this model. The point being that counterintuitively, it is much easier to fluctuate a whole new universe than it is a brain. And partly that's because brains are big, right? You know, brains are a few pounds and a lot of particles and they're very complex, very specific systems. A universe, you might think, "Wow, a universe has even more particles and is even more specific." But that's not true. This is the beauty of general relativity and inflationary cosmology. You can make a baby universe that is not much bigger than the Planck scale and is completely featureless. Has zero particles in it. Just has energy. And through the power of inflation, inflation can expand that Planck scale thing to a macroscopic size, and then through the power of quantum mechanics and reheating, it can turn that energy into a large number of particles. So, if you just do the math, the number of new universes you will make is much much larger than the number of Boltzmann brains you will make.

segments.questionText: Could you explain what quantum error correction entails?
segments.answerText: entails I'm not sure how much detail you want here so I'll try to give you know the very basic idea there's something called classical error correction which is probably a good idea to understand first and this is the idea let's say you have a string of bits let's say you have one bit okay so you have a bit that is either 0 or 1 and you want to send it to your friend but you send it over a wire and there's noise on the wire and there's always the possibility that the bit just gets flipped there's some probability that that happens just by accident so how can you guard against that classically what you can do is you can duplicate that bit if it's a 0 or a 1 if it's 0 you turn that one 0 into 5 zeroes you send all 5 if it's a 1 you turn into 5 ones and you send all 5 of those and then when that your receiver gets the message it is correspondingly less likely that all of the bits have flipped so you just do some majority count and presume that that was the message that you tried to send it's never a hundred percent reliable but you can increase the reliability as much as you want there's a couple of problems with that quantum mechanically one is that it's you can't Xerox quantum mechanical bits if someone gives you a bit and says don't measure it just duplicate it that is literally impossible to do in quantum mechanics there is a no-cloning theorem that says you can't do that you can manipulate it in different ways but you can't just make many many copies of it and send all those copies the other problem is when you do measure a quantum you only get to measure at once before it disappears right because you've destroyed it by by measuring it you've changed its nature because its wavefunction has collapsed so what you end up doing is there's a way of taking one bit and distributing the information sort of basically what you're doing you're diluting the bit you're not xeroxing it many times but you're diluting in this in the way that if it's some linear combination of 0 & 1 you make a linear combination of 0 0 0 0 & 1 1 1 1 ok so you're distributing that combination over many bits and then it turns out the clever thing is you can separately ask you can separately figure out are all the bits that the receiver receives the same or has one of them flipped ok and if they're all receiving the same one then you presume that ok it probably came through okay and that's a way that you can actually do quantum error correction you can you can make sure that it's very unlikely that you're that all the bits were being flipped now there is one interesting thing that is worth mentioning here is that that kind of process makes use of a hidden or maybe not so hidden assumption which is that if you have physical bits like electron spins qubits quantum bits so like electron spins or entanglement between multiple particles or whatever certain kinds of errors are more likely than other kinds of errors it is more likely to change one bit at a time by that bit being hit by a cosmic ray or whatever then all the all the bits changing a little bit but in concert ok so that's what makes quantum error correction possible I hope that was some help this is a absolutely very central stuff for making practical quantum

segments.answerText: know what space and time really are well I you know my personal belief that Kant was just totally wrong about this I mean Convery famously and as we will talk about briefly in an upcoming podcast had an idea of the synthetic a priori that there was something that could be a priori true but was not true just by the by the consequence of the definitions of the terms in the statement and so I don't actually agree that there are such things as synthetic a priori I also don't agree separately that there's anything really aa priori in our minds about space and time I think it's a failure of imagination to think that we need space and time to perceive the world I think that we have space in time and we use them to perceive the world but I'm open to the possibility that if space and time were very different than what we know them to be there would still be something that we could imagine calling perceiving the world so I and I also think that it as a as a matter of how science gets done you know this idea of perceiving things as they are is never how science gets done and by the 20th century we are very familiar with our work from cosmology and relativity and quantum mechanics and field theory that our theories that we construct to explain the world are very very distant from our everyday experiences of the world and the reason that we're able to construct them is because we go back and forth between theory and experiment and ask what kinds of theoretical predictions match the data etc so it's not about you know perceiving directly the reality it's about figuring out the reality by the basis of this back and forth scientific method and I see no reason whatsoever to think that our scientific method will not succeed at telling us what space and time really are that's my optimistic take Peter

segments.questionText: Wojiech Zurek in his “existential interpretation” of QM argues, that although the Universe as a whole is a quantum wavefunction, once we select one classical world as our observed branch, there’s no reason to treat other branches as equally real. In fact, there is only this branch, and the rest of the universe is simply in the mixed quantum state. Such argument seems to avoid some philosophical problems of many-worlds, where multiple persons exist in different branches. While I understand that branching is in principle a matter of convenience, why do you treat other branches as equally real.
segments.answerText: is you know the if you want to argue well I don't need to treat the rest of the wavefunction the universe as real I would say why what is your reason not for treating it as real right like I don't need to treat the other side of Los Angeles as real you know it just exists in my mind maybe it becomes real when I go visit but I don't need to treat it as real but obviously it's just way easier to treat it as real it's just a simpler explanation for what's going on in reality if I write down a wave function where a priori there's a bunch of pieces of it and they all look equally real and then someone puts their finger on one of the terms in the wavefunction says yes but that's where I live I don't see why suddenly all the rest of the wave function becomes less important and less real right and in fact the argument about the probability and the born rule goes the other way you need multiple persons in other branches to understand where probability comes from Zurich has an argument for probability but I think he's cheating basically zero X argument for probability comes down to the fact that mathematically the wave function squared is the only sensible thing that you could apply to probability in many worlds and that's certainly true but that's kind of obviously trivially true everyone's always known that everyone always knew that if you got a probability rule out of many worlds it would be the wave function squared that's not the surprise the important question is why is there anything worthy of the term probability at all and I think in my personal view is that in order to answer that question you need to take the other worlds seriously because I think that the probability is a credence you put on the self locating uncertainty attached to all those different observers and all

segments.questionText: Assume two absolutely identical universes spring into existence at the exact same moment. Identical, that is, except for one fluke difference: biology emerges in Universe A some eight billion years into the picture and continues for at least billions of years. Which universe experiences heat death first? Does biology retard or accelerate entropy?
segments.answerText: experiences heat death first this biology or accelerate entropy I think the biology accelerates the rate at which entropy is increasing biology depends on the fact that there is low entropy around that there is free energy to be taken advantage of and biology takes advantage of it by converting it into higher entropy energy by getting rid of the free energy and turning into waste heat and so forth so just at a qualitative level biology would certainly increase the rate of entropy production now realistically it's a tiny fraction right I mean the Sun is doing a way better job but increasing the entropy of the universe then the earth and the black hole at the center of our galaxy is doing a way way way better job at increasing the entropy of the universe than all of the stars in the Milky Way so life does not play a very crucial role in our actually universe but in principle it speeds up the approach

segments.questionText: Why isn’t the 2nd law of thermodynamics considered to be ‘a’, if not ‘the’ solution to the 1st law? [considered here on the scale of the Universe, i.e. the ‘Big Picture’]
segments.answerText: scale of the universe ie the big picture so I'm not sure what to say because I'm not sure exactly you mean by the phrase the solution to the first law the first law of thermodynamics says energy is conserved it does not involve the word entropy in any way the second law says that entropy only increases or stays constant in closed systems there's other versions of it but that's the basic idea I think that these are two independent statements I can imagine worlds in which energy is not conserved but the second law is still true I can imagine worlds in which the first law is true energy still conserved but the second law is not true for example a world in thermal equilibrium or a world with just like one simple harmonic oscillator rocking back and forth where the entropy is just not defined but energy is still conserved so I really think that these are separate things that work together to give us the world of thermodynamics
